narration.text: Imagine you're looking for a word in a dictionary. You could start at page one and read every single entry until you find it. Or, you could flip to the middle, see if your word is before or after, and keep halving the search. Both approaches work, but one is dramatically faster. That difference is exactly what Big O notation measures.

Big O notation describes how an algorithm's performance scales as the input grows. It answers the question: if I double the data, how much longer will this take? Let's walk through the most important complexity classes, from fastest to slowest.

First up: O of 1, constant time. This means the operation takes the same amount of time no matter how big the input is. Think of looking up a value in an array by its index. Whether the array has ten items or ten million, jumping directly to position five is instant. It's like knowing the exact page number in a book — you just flip right to it.

Next: O of log n, logarithmic time. This is what binary search does. Each step cuts the problem in half. Searching a sorted list of one million items takes only about twenty steps. Double the data, and you add just one more step. It's the dictionary trick — flip to the middle, eliminate half, repeat.

O of n, linear time, means you touch every element once. Searching an unsorted list for a specific value requires checking each item. If you double the list, it takes roughly twice as long. Think of reading every page in a book to find a particular sentence.
Now let's check your understanding so far.

O of n log n is where efficient sorting algorithms live. Merge sort and quicksort both achieve this. They divide the data into halves recursively, then merge the results. For a million items, that's about twenty million operations — much better than the alternative we'll see next.

O of n squared, quadratic time, is what happens with naive sorting algorithms like bubble sort. Every element gets compared to every other element. Double the input, and the work quadruples. For a thousand items, that's a million comparisons. For a million items? A trillion. This is where things start to get painful.

Finally, O of 2 to the n, exponential time. This is the danger zone. Each additional input element doubles the total work. Computing Fibonacci numbers recursively without caching is a classic example. For just 30 items, you're looking at over a billion operations. For 50? More operations than atoms in the human body.
Let's put this into perspective with a slider. Watch how different complexities scale as n grows.
Here's a practical question to tie it all together.

To summarize: Big O strips away constants and lower-order terms to focus on the growth pattern. O of 1 is instant. O of log n barely grows. O of n grows proportionally. O of n log n is the sweet spot for sorting. O of n squared gets slow fast. And O of 2 to the n is practically unusable for large inputs. When you're choosing an algorithm, Big O tells you which one will still be standing when the data gets big.

q-lookup.title: Array Access
q-lookup.desc: You have a sorted array of 1 million names. What's the Big O of finding a name by its exact index position?
options: O(log n) — it's a sorted structure
O(1) — direct index lookup is constant time
O(n) — you might need to scan the whole array

correct.title: Exactly right
correct.body: Index-based access jumps straight to the memory location. The array size doesn't matter — it's always one step.

wrong.title: Not quite
wrong.body: Searching for a value would require O(log n) or O(n), but accessing by index is O(1) — you go directly to the position in memory.

try-sort-complexities.title: Order the Complexities
try-sort-complexities.desc: Drag to arrange from fastest to slowest growth rate.

correct.body: Perfect! This ordering is the backbone of algorithm analysis. Memorize it and you'll instantly know which algorithms are practical at scale.

wrong.body: The correct order from fastest to slowest: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2ⁿ). Each jump is a dramatic increase in growth rate.

try-categorize-algos.title: Match Algorithm to Complexity
try-categorize-algos.desc: Drag each algorithm into its Big O category.

correct.body: Nailed it! Knowing which complexity class an algorithm falls into helps you pick the right tool for the job.

wrong.body: Array/hash lookups are O(1). Merge sort and quicksort average O(n log n). Bubble sort and selection sort compare every pair, giving O(n²).

try-slider-scale.title: How Fast Does It Grow?
try-slider-scale.desc: Slide to change the input size and see how many operations each complexity requires.

stops.label: n = 10
stops.detail: O(n²) = 100 ops, O(n log n) ≈ 33 ops. Both feel instant at this size.

stops.label: n = 30
stops.detail: O(n²) = 900 ops, O(n log n) ≈ 147 ops. Quadratic is 6× more work.

stops.label: n = 50
stops.detail: O(n²) = 2,500 ops, O(n log n) ≈ 282 ops. The gap is nearly 9×.

stops.label: n = 100
stops.detail: O(n²) = 10,000 ops, O(n log n) ≈ 664 ops. Quadratic does 15× more work. Now imagine n = 1,000,000.

q-practical.title: Real-World Choice
q-practical.desc: You need to find duplicates in a list of 10 million user IDs. Which approach will finish in reasonable time?
options: Sort first, then scan for neighbors — O(n log n)
Check one random pair and hope for the best

correct.title: Smart choice
correct.body: Sorting first at O(n log n) then scanning at O(n) is practical even for millions of items. The O(n²) approach would take roughly 50 trillion comparisons.

wrong.title: Think about scale
wrong.body: At 10 million items, O(n²) means 100 trillion operations — that could take hours or days. Sorting first at O(n log n) keeps it manageable in seconds.
